coredata.dc:title: DNA-linked CdSe QDs/AGQDs “Z-scheme” system: Ultrasensitive and highly selective photoelectrochemical sensing platform with negative background signal
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Leixia
Xiao, Ke
Zhang, Xiaohua
Du, Cuicui
Chen, Jinhua
coredata.dc:description: Depended on the target-induced formation of a DNA-linked CdSe quantum dots/amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (denoted as CdSe QDs/AGQDs) “Z-scheme” system, an ultrasensitive and highly selective PEC sensing platform has been constructed for DNA assay. Firstly, the indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode was modified with the poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA)-decorated reduced graphene oxide (PDDA-rGO) to fix and disperse CdSe QDs. The obtained ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs electrode generated an anode photocurrent. After catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) and hybridization chain reaction (HCR) processes triggered by target DNA (T-DNA), abundant and long double-strand DNA (dsDNA) were introduced to the sensing platform. And then, numerous AGQDs were intercalated into the dsDNA strands, resulting in the formation of DNA-linked CdSe QDs/AGQDs “Z-scheme” system and the change of the response signal from anodic to cathode photocurrents. Because of the negative background signal and DNA-based amplification strategy, T-DNA was detected sensitively (linear range, 1 aM - 100 pM; detectable limit, 0.1 aM). Importantly, the developed PEC sensing platform can avoid the effects of the initial signal and background noise, and has the enhanced sensitivity and anti-interference ability, which make it may have promising application in bioanalysis and disease diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Z-scheme
CdSe QDs
Graphene QDs
DNA
PEC biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the PEC sensing platform
Characterization of CdSe QDs and AGQDs
Characterization of the PEC sensing platform
Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the PEC sensing platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by NSFC ( 21727810 , 21475035 ), and the Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of NSFC ( 21521063 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the PEC sensing platform for T-DNA assay depended on the DNA-linked CdSe QDs/AGQDs “Z-scheme” system with negative background signal.
(A) TEM image and (B) XRD pattern of CdSe QDs. (C) TEM and (D) AFM images of AGQDs.
SEM images of (A) bare ITO, (B) ITO/PDDA-rGO and (C) ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs electrodes.

(A) Nyquist plots of the different photoelectrodes in 0.1 M KCl aqueous solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3− and 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4-. Amplitude, 5 mV; frequency, 0.01 Hz −100 kHz. ITO/PDDA-rGO electrode (a), ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs (b), ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP (c), ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP/MCH (d), ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP/MCH/HP1/HP2 (e), ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP/MCH/HP1/HP2/HP3/HP4 (f), ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP/MCH/HP1/HP2/HP3/HP4/AGQDs (g). (B) Photocurrent signals of the different photoelectrodes in 10 mM Tris−HCl (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M AA at -0.1 V. ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs electrode (a), ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP (b), ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP/MCH (c), ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP/MCH/HP1/HP2 (d), ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP/MCH/HP1/HP2/HP3/HP4 (e), ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP/MCH/HP1/HP2/ HP3/HP4/AGQDs (f).

Optimization of experimental parameters on the photocurrents of the developed PEC sensor. (A) Incubation time for CHA process (incubation time for HCR process, 2 h; AGQDs, 40 μL), (B) incubation time for HCR process (incubation time for CHA process, 2 h; AGQDs, 40 μL) and (C) the added volume of AGQDs solution (incubation time for CHA process, 2 h; incubation time for HCR process, 2 h). The concentration of T-DNA is 100 fM.

(A) Photocurrent signals of the developed PEC sensing platform towards different concentrations of T-DNA: 0, 0.1 aM, 0.5 aM, 1 aM, 5 aM, 10 aM,100 aM, 1 fM, 10 fM, 100 fM, 1 pM, 10 pM, 100 pM, 1 nM, 10 nM (from a to o). (B) The relationship between the photocurrent value and the concentration of T-DNA.
(A) Selectivity of the established PEC sensing platform towards N-DNA, Tm-DNA, Sm-DNA and T-DNA. (B) Reproducibility of the developed PEC sensor in the same batch. The concentration of DNA is 100 fM.
The DNA sequences used in this work.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aThe sequences with wavy line in CP and HP1, the sequences with underline in HP1 and HP2, the italic sequences in HP2 and HP3, the sequences with underline in HP3 and HP4, and the sequences with wavy line in HP3 and HP4 are complementary, respectively. The bold sequences in the same oligonucleotide are complementary. The italic bases in T-DNA and HP1 are complementary. The bases with underline in Tm-DNA and Sm-DNA refer to the mismatched bases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different methods for HTLV-II DNA assay.
Recovery assay for T-DNA in 10-fold diluted human serum samples based on the proposed PEC sensing platform.

head.ce:title: DNA-linked CdSe QDs/AGQDs “Z-scheme” system: Ultrasensitive and highly selective photoelectrochemical sensing platform with negative background signal

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, 410082, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Depended on the target-induced formation of a DNA-linked CdSe quantum dots/amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (denoted as CdSe QDs/AGQDs) “Z-scheme” system, an ultrasensitive and highly selective PEC sensing platform has been constructed for DNA assay. Firstly, the indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode was modified with the poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA)-decorated reduced graphene oxide (PDDA-rGO) to fix and disperse CdSe QDs. The obtained ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs electrode generated an anode photocurrent. After catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) and hybridization chain reaction (HCR) processes triggered by target DNA (T-DNA), abundant and long double-strand DNA (dsDNA) were introduced to the sensing platform. And then, numerous AGQDs were intercalated into the dsDNA strands, resulting in the formation of DNA-linked CdSe QDs/AGQDs “Z-scheme” system and the change of the response signal from anodic to cathode photocurrents. Because of the negative background signal and DNA-based amplification strategy, T-DNA was detected sensitively (linear range, 1 aM - 100 pM; detectable limit, 0.1 aM). Importantly, the developed PEC sensing platform can avoid the effects of the initial signal and background noise, and has the enhanced sensitivity and anti-interference ability, which make it may have promising application in bioanalysis and disease diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging analytical technique which totally separates the input source (light energy) and output signal (such as photocurrent), photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor has received more and more attention in recent years [1–10]. In general PEC assays, photoactive materials were directly modified on the sensing interface to get a remarkable initial PEC signals [11–13]. When the target was added, the PEC signal amplifier (enhancer or quencher) was introduced to generate PEC signal differences, thus, the target was quantified [14–16]. The analytical properties of the PEC biosensors rely mainly on the photoelectric performance of the photo-active materials. As one of the most important parameters, effective separation of photogenerated electrons and photogenerated holes is a key issue to get excellent photoelectric properties of the photo-active materials. Till now, many strategies, which mainly used various noble metals (Au, Ag, etc.), semiconductors or organic materials as the sensitizers, have been developed to depress the electron-hole recombination [17–22]. Recently, a new strategy, “Z-scheme” heterojunctions, have aroused great interest because of the particular charge-carrier transfer path that likes a letter “Z”, possessing high efficacy for spatially separating the charge carriers and enhancing the redox ability of the heterojunction [23,24]. Taking advantage of the “Z-scheme” heterojunctions, some PEC biosensors were constructed [25,26]. For instance, Wang et al. proposed a label-free photocathodic immunosensor for carcino embryonic antigen (CEA) detection by introducing “Z-scheme” I-BiOCl/CdS as highly effective photoelectric material [25]. Liu et al. developed a “Z-scheme” type CdTe-Bi2S3 heterojunction with improved visible-light-driven photoelectrical conversion efficiency for sensitive detection of microcystin-LR [26]. Due to the low recombination between photogenerated electrons and photogenerated holes of the “Z-scheme” systems, a large photocurrent response were obtained. However, the initial signal and background noise are also inevitably enlarged by the “Z-scheme” systems, which would bring about low sensitivity and restricted detection limit during the detection of target.

Recently, a new “Z-scheme” system (CdSe quantum dots/amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (denoted as CdSe QDs/AGQDs)) has been developed in our group [27]. The CdSe QDs modified ITO electrode had an obvious anodic photocurrent. However, after the generation of CdSe QDs/AGQDs “Z-scheme” system, the photocurrent polarity switched and a large cathodic photocurrent generated. This interesting phenomenon inspires us to develop a new PEC sensing platform to solve the above issues on initial signal and background noise.

Here, taking Human T-cell lymphotropic virus type II DNA (HTLV-II DNA), which can considerably influence many diseases [28–30], as a model, a DNA-linked CdSe QDs/AGQDs “Z-scheme” system was designed to construct an ultrasensitive and highly selective PEC sensing platform with negative background signal for DNA assay (Scheme 1 ). Firstly, the indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode was modified with the positive-charged reduced graphene oxide which was decorated with poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA) (denoted as PDDA-rGO) to fix and disperse CdSe QDs. The obtained ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs electrode generated an anode photocurrent. After catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) and hybridization chain reaction (HCR) processes triggered by target DNA (T-DNA), abundant and long double-strand DNA (dsDNA) were introduced to the sensing platform. And then, numerous AGQDs were intercalated into the dsDNA strands [31], resulting in the formation of DNA-linked CdSe QDs/AGQDs “Z-scheme” system and the change of the response signal from anodic to cathodic photocurrents. Based on the intensity of the cathodic photocurrent, the T-DNA was quantified. Due to the target-induced photocurrent-polarity switching, the developed PEC sensing platform features with negative background signal. Thus, the effects of initial signal and background noise on the analytical performance of the PEC sensor could be completely eliminated, which would endow the developed PEC sensor high sensitivity and excellent anti-interferent ability, and promising applications in bioanalysis and disease diagnosis.

Cadmium chloride hemipentahydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O), sodiumborohydride (NaBH4), ascorbic acid (AA), ammonia solution and tris-(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Graphene oxide solution (GO, 5 mg mL−1) prepared by Hummers method was obtained from NewMater New Nanotechnology Co., Ltd. (Guangyuan, China). Hydrazinium hydrate (H4N2·H2O, 80 wt%) were purchased from Shanpu Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA) (MW = 200,000–350,000, 20 wt%, in water) and 6-mercaptohexanol (MCH) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (USA). Selenium metal powder (Se), mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 98 %) were ordered from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris (2-carboxyethyl) phosphinehydrochloride (TCEP) were provided by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The normal human serum was supplied by Anyan Trade Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The indium tin oxide (ITO) glass was supplied by Zhuhai Kaivo Electronic Components Co., Ltd. (China). All chemicals (analytical grade) were directly used. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used throughout the whole experiments, which was obtained from a Millipore system (Millipore Corp., Bedford, MA).All the synthetic DNAs were supplied by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The DNA sequences are shown in Table 1 .

The morphology and structure of the obtained materials were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, Holland) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JSM-6700 F, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded on an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained on a Dimension Icon atomic force microscope (Bruker, USA). A PLS-SXE 300 Xe lamp fitted with a 420 nm UV filter was utilized as the irradiation source. The electrochemical and PEC tests were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D, Chenhua Instrumental Co., Shanghai, China) with a typical three-electrode configuration at ambient temperature. The modified ITO slice was acted as the working electrode. A Pt wire was employed as the auxiliary electrode, and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was utilized as the reference electrode. 10 mM Tris−HCl (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M ascorbic acid (AA) was utilized as the electrolyte solution.

The reduced graphene oxide, which was decorated with poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA) (denoted as PDDA-rGO), was prepared via the in-situ reduction of GO in the presence of PDDA as reported literature with minor modification [32]. First, 90 mL ultrapure water and 10 mL GO solution were mixed. After sonication for 30 min, 0.5 mL of 20 wt% PDDA was injected, and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, 0.5 mL of 80 wt% hydrazine hydrate was injected and the solution was stirred for 24 h at 90 °C. Eventually, PDDA-rGO was obtained by filtration and washed, and then re-dispersed in ultrapure water for later use.

CdSe QDs were prepared according to the following procedure [33]. As the precursor, NaHSe was obtained via dissolving 7.6 mg NaBH4 into 5 mL H2O under nitrogen gas, then dissolving 7.9 mg Se powder into the solution. After 45.7 mg CdCl2·2.5H2O were added in 50 mL ultrapure water and stirred at 100 °C under nitrogen atmosphere, 44 μL MPA was injected. Then, the pH was regulated to 11 with 1 M NaOH solution. After 30 min, freshly prepared NaHSe was quickly added into the above solution. The clear orange solution of MPA-CdSe QDs was obtained by reacting for 4 h under nitrogen gas flow. At last, the production was extracted by ethanol thrice, and then centrifuged. The purified CdSe QDs were re-dispersed in ultrapure water and kept in the dark at 4 °C.

The GQDs were prepared based on the reported method [34]. The amino functionalized GQDs (AGQDs) were obtained through a hydrothermal route. Briefly, 6 mL of 28 wt% ammonia solution was added in 6 mL of the prepared GQDs aqueous solution and mixed in a 50 mL round bottom flask. After stirring for 1 h, the above solution was poured in the 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. Then, for removing the excessive ammonia, light yellow supernatant was heated to 100 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, the solution was further purified through dialysis. The obtained AGQDs solution was stored in the dark at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the PEC sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Before modification, ITO electrodes were sonicated successively in acetone, ethanol/1 M sodium hydroxide solution (1:1, v/v) and ultrapure water. After ITO electrode was dried for 2 h at 60 °C, 20 μL of 0.1 mg mL−1 PDDA-rGO suspension was dropped onto it and dried in air. Then, 20 μL of 0.5 mg mL−1 CdSe QDs were coated on the electrode. Subsequently, 20 μL of 1 μM capture probe DNA (CP) activated in 10 mM Tris−HCl (pH 7.4) involving 10 mM TCEP and 0.1 M NaCl for 1 h was dropped on the ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs electrode for 12 h at 4 °C for the immobilization of CP through S-Cd bond. After washed with 10 mM Tris−HCl (pH 7.4) three times to remove any weakly absorbed CP, the electrode was incubated with 20 μL of 2 mM MCH for 1 h to block the possible bare part of the electrode, then rinsed with 10 mM Tris−HCl (pH 7.4).

Prior to use, the hairpins probe DNA strands (HP1, HP2, HP3 and HP4) were annealed at 95 °C for 5 min and slowly cooled down to ambient temperature. Then, CHA reaction was conducted in a mixture containing 1 μM HP1 and 1 μM HP2 (both of HP1 and HP2 were prepared in 20 mM Tris−HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 5 mM MgCl2, 100 mM NaCl)) and different concentrations of T-DNA prepared in 10 mM Tris−HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mM MgCl2, 100 mM NaCl). After the incubation of this mixture was carried out for 2 h at 37 °C, the above modified electrode was incubated with 20 μL of the mixture for 2 h at 37 °C, then washed with 10 mM Tris−HCl (pH 7.4) three times. Afterwards, the electrode was incubated with 20 μL of 10 mM Tris−HCl solution (pH 7.4, 1 mM MgCl2, 500 mM NaCl) containing 3 μM HP3 and 3 μM HP4 for 2 h at 37 °C to carry out HCR reaction, and washed with 10 mM Tris−HCl (pH 7.4) three times. Consequently, abundant and long dsDNAs were formed on the electrode surface. After that, 30 μL of the as-prepared AGQDs was dripped onto the sensing platform for 2 h at ambient temperature. The obtained ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP/MCH/HP1/HP2/HP3/HP4/AGQDs electrode was rinsed carefully with 10 mM Tris−HCl (pH 7.4) three times. The photocurrent signal of the obtained electrode was measured in 10 mM Tris−HCl (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M AA at -0.1 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CdSe QDs and AGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained CdSe QDs were investigated by high resolution TEM (HRTEM) and XRD. From Fig. 1 A, HRTEM image clearly shows that CdSe QDs have a mean size of 4.4 nm and a interplanar distance of 0.33 nm corresponding to the (111) facet. For the XRD pattern of CdSe QDs (Fig. 1B), the peaks at 25.8°, 42.4° and 49.1° are observed, which relate to the diffractions of the (111), (220), and (311) planes of the cubic phase of CdSe (JCPDS No. 19-0191), respectively.

As displayed in Fig. 1C, the as-prepared AGQDs have an average diameter of 3.0 nm and a lattice parameter of 0.21 nm corresponding to (102) plane of graphene. Moreover, the AFM image (Fig. 1D) with corresponding height profile shows that the average height of the AGQDs is around 0.93 nm, suggesting the as-prepared AGQDs may be bi-layered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the PEC sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs, ITO/PDDA-rGO and bare ITO electrodes were investigated (Fig. 2 ). Different from the bare ITO slice (Fig. 2A), the PDDA-rGO modified ITO electrode has smooth surface (Fig. 2B), which should be contributed from the sheet morphology of PDDA-rGO with very thin thickness (about 1.09 nm, Fig. S1). Compared Fig. 2C with Fig. 2B, it is clear that CdSe QDs are successfully coated on the surface of ITO/PDDA-rGO electrode. In addition, the C, S, N, Se, O and Cd elements disperse uniformly on the PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs modified ITO electrode (Fig. S2), indicating that CdSe QDs are successfully assembled on the electrode surface with uniform dispersion thanks to the electrostatic attraction between negative-charged CdSe QDs and positive-charged PDDA-rGO.

The different stages for the preparation of the PEC sensing platform were monitored by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The semicircular section of EIS represents the interfacial charge-transfer resistance (R ct) of the electrode. From Fig. 3 A, the ITO/PDDA-rGO electrode shows an straight line with almost no semicircular portion (curve a), indicating extraordinary electron transport property of PDDA-rGO. Modification of CdSe QDs on the electrode surface results in the increase of R ct value (curve b), ascribing to the electrostatic repulsion action between negative-charged CdSe QDs and redox probe ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4−) and the semiconductor's property of CdSe QDs. Subsequently, the successive modifications of CP, MCH, HP1/HP2 (T-DNA, 100 fM), and HP3/HP4 give rise to the increase of R ct values (curves c to f), due to the blocking effect and/or electrostatic repulsion action resulting from MCH and DNA strands. However, the intercalation of AGQDs into the dsDNA strands decreases the R ct value of the electrode because of the good electrical conductivity of the AGQDs (curve g). All of the above discussion suggests the successful construction of the PEC sensing platform.

Furthermore, the preparation process of the PEC sensing platform was step-by-step characterized by PEC method. In Fig. 3B, an anodic photocurrent is observed on the ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs electrode (curve a, -9.567 μA). Because of the steric hindrance effects, the successive immobilizations of CP, MCH, HP1/HP2, and HP3/HP4 result in the successive decreases of the anodic photocurrents (curves b to e). However, after the intercalation of AGQDs into the dsDNA strands on the PEC sensing platform [31], the DNA-linked CdSe QDs/AGQDs “Z-scheme” system is formed, resulting in the switching of photocurrent polarity and the generation of a large cathodic photocurrent (curve f, 12.86 μA). These results imply that the established PEC sensing platform can be utilized to detect T-DNA on the basis of negative background signal.

According to the above results, the possible photocurrent generation mechanism of the developed PEC sensing platform is also presented in Scheme 1. Under visible light irradiation, both CdSe QDs and AGQDs are simultaneously excited to generate photogenerated electrons and holes at conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), respectively. The electrons at the CB of CdSe QDs transfer to the VB of AGQDs through dsDNA strands because of the matched energy levels between CdSe QDs and AGQDs. Then, the photogenerated electrons at the CB of AGQDs can be captured by dissolved O2. The photogenerated holes at the VB of CdSe QDs are used to oxidize AA. The above electron transfer route obeys “Z-scheme” mechanism, leading to the efficient separation of photogenerated electrons and photogenerated holes. Hence, an enhanced cathodic photocurrent is achieved.

The incubation times for CHA and HCR processes, and the added amount of AGQDs can affect the analytical properties of the constructed PEC sensing platform. Therefore, these parameters should be optimized.

The influence of the incubation time for CHA process on the photocurrent of the PEC sensing platform is displayed in Fig. 4 A. When the incubation time increases from 0.5 h to 2 h, the photocurrent increases. After 2 h, a plateau reaches, indicating that 2 h can be utilized as the optimal incubation time for CHA process.

The incubation time for HCR reaction is another crucial factor influencing the analysis properties of the PEC sensing platform. From Fig. 4B, when the incubation time increases, the photocurrent increases and then reaches a plateau at 2 h. So, 2 h is selected as the optimal incubation time for HCR process.

Fig. 4C shows the effect of the added amount of AGQDs on the photocurrent of the PEC sensor. It is noted that the photocurrent increases with the increase of the added volume of AGQDs solution. When the added volume of AGQDs solution is more than 30 μL, no obvious change of the photocurrent can be observed. Therefore, 30 μL is employed as the appropriate added volume of AGQDs solution.

Under the optimal experimental parameters, the proposed PEC sensing platform was employed to quantitatively detect T-DNA (Fig. 5 A). When the T-DNA concentration (C T-DNA) is 0, an anodic photocurrent is observed to be -5.964 μA. In the presence of 0.1 aM T-DNA, the photocurrent polarity is changed and a cathodic photocurrent (0.7916 μA) can be obtained. This indicates that a PEC biosensor with negative background signal is established successfully. It is noted that the total photocurrent response is 6.7556 μA, and much larger than the related cathodic photocurrent (0.7916 μA), implying that the developed PEC sensing platform has ultrahigh sensitivity due to the negative background signal of the PEC biosensor. As displayed in Fig. 5A, the cathodic photocurrent increases with the increase of C T-DNA. A linear relationship between the cathodic photocurrent and the logarithm of C T-DNA is obtained in the range from 1 aM to 100 pM (Fig. 5B). The linear equation is I (μA) = 2.295 logC T-DNA (fM) + 8.271 (R2 = 0.9979). The detectable limit is 0.1 aM, which is much lower than the calculated detection limit obtained from previously reported methods (Table 2 ). Importantly, because only the target can result in the generation of cathodic photocurrent, the PEC sensor can completely eliminate the effects of initial signal and background noise.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the PEC sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is one of the key issues of a sensing platform in potential applications. As seen in Fig. 6 A, for single-base mismatched DNA (Sm-DNA), three-base mismatched DNA (Tm-DNA), noncomplementary DNA (N-DNA), all the anodic photocurrents are observed. However, only for T-DNA, a cathodic photocurrent generates. This indicates clearly that the proposed PEC sensing platform has an excellent selectivity for T-DNA assay, which is attributed to the excellent characteristics of the proposed PEC sensing platform with negative background signal (the different photocurrent polarities between background signal and target-induced response signal).

In addition, the ITO/PDDA-rGO/CdSe QDs/CP/MCH electrode was kept at 4 °C for 14 days, and the photocurrent signal only attenuated about 2.14 % of the primary signal for 100 fM T-DNA detection, demonstrating the satisfactory stability of the PEC sensing platform. For the further exploration of the reproducibility, five modified ITO slices were measured with the same concentration of T-DNA (100 fM) under the same conditions. As displayed in Fig. 6B, the relative standard deviation (RSD) is 2.17 %, indicating an acceptable reproducibility.

The practical applicability of the established PEC sensing platform was investigated via detecting T-DNA in the 10-fold diluted (10 mM Tris−HCl, pH 7.4) human serum samples. Different concentrations of T-DNA were spiked into the diluted human serum samples. The recovery was in the range of 96.58 %–106.98 %, as displayed in Table 3 . The above results indicate that the developed PEC sensing platform have satisfactory recovery and great potentiality to detect T-DNA in complex biological system.

In summary, taking HTLV-II DNA as a model, a DNA-linked CdSe QDs/AGQDs “Z-scheme” system was designed to construct an ultrasensitive and highly selective PEC sensing platform with negative background signal to detect DNA. Depended on the excellent characteristics of negative background signal and DNA-based amplification strategy, the developed PEC sensing platform has excellent analytical performance: a linear relationship between the cathodic photocurrent and the logarithm of T-DNA concentration is obtained in the range from 1 aM to 100 pM. The detectable limit is 0.1 aM, which is much lower than the calculated detection limit obtained from the previously reported methods. Also, the developed PEC biosensor has an excellent selectivity for T-DNA assay. Furthermore, the stability, reproducibility and recovery of the proposed sensing platform are satisfactory. These make it may have promising application in bioanalysis and disease diagnosis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by NSFC (21727810, 21475035), and the Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of NSFC (21521063).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.127480.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual functional molecular imprinted polymer-modified organometal lead halide perovskite: synthesis and application for photoelectrochemical sensing of salicylic acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yang, Y. Gao, Z. Ji, L. Zhu, C. Yang, Y. Zhao, Y. Shu, D. Jin, Q. Xu, W. Zhao, Dual functional molecular imprinted polymer-modified organometal lead halide perovskite: synthesis and application for photoelectrochemical sensing of salicylic acid, Anal. Chem. 91 (2019) 9356-9360.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical immunoassay for matrix metalloproteinase-2 detection based on CdS:Mn/CdTe cosensitized TiO2 nanotubes and signal amplification of SiO2@Ab2 conjugates

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new photoelectrochemical biosensor for ultrasensitive determination of nucleic acids based on a three-stage cascade signal amplification strategy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Xiong, X. Yan, X. Zhang, Y. Li, R. Yang, L. Meng, J. Chen, A new photoelectrochemical biosensor for ultrasensitive determination of nucleic acids based on a three-stage cascade signal amplification strategy, Analyst 143 (2018) 2799–2806.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Leixia Meng studying for a Ph.D. degree in State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, China, working under the supervision of Prof. Jinhua Chen. Her main research interests are photoelectrochemical biosensors.

Ke Xiao is studying for a Master’s degree in State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, China, working under the supervision of Prof. Xiaohua Zhang. His main research interests are electrochemical biosensors.

Xiaohua Zhang is a professor in Hunan University, China. She received a Ph.D. degree in applied chemistry from Hunan University, China in 2006. Her main research interests are electrochemical biosensors and electrocatalysis.

Cuicui Du is an assistant professor in Hunan University, China. She received a Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Jilin University, China in 2018. Her main research interests are electrochemical biosensors and electrocatalysis.

Jinhua Chen is a professor of chemistry in Hunan University, China. He received a Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Hunan University, China in 1997. The main research interests of Professor Chen are electrochemical biosensors, energy storage and conversion, and electrocatalysis.
